name: glazed carrots
instructions:
Classic braise-and-glaze technique for carrots. Two variants, structurally opposed on several axes:
- VARIANT A (Julia Child): butter + granulated sugar + WATER. French classical, herb finish is parsley or tarragon, cut rounds/oblique, no acid.
- VARIANT B (modern rustic, vegan-friendly): OIL + brown sugar + balsamic vinegar + dried thyme, cooked in vegetable STOCK. Cut lengthwise (halves), rainbow carrots visually striking.

Pick based on what you're serving alongside — variant A pairs cleanly with french-classical mains (roast chicken, coq au vin, chicken with vinegar); variant B fits better with modern/rustic plates and vegan menus.

=== VARIANT A — JULIA CHILD'S CAROTTES VICHY / ÉTUVÉES AU BEURRE (BUTTER + SUGAR) ===

From Mastering the Art of French Cooking. The canonical classical method: carrots simmer in butter + sugar + water until tender, then the water cooks off and the butter-and-sugar residue glazes the carrots as they finish.

INGREDIENTS:
- 1 lb carrots, peeled and cut into rounds or oblique-cut chunks (~1/4 to 1/2 inch)
- 2-3 tbsp unsalted butter
- 1-2 tsp granulated sugar (small — this is a glaze, not a candy)
- 1/2 tsp salt
- water, just enough to cover the carrots by ~1/2 inch
- (optional) chopped parsley or tarragon at the finish

PROCEDURE:
1. Place the cut carrots in a wide saucepan or skillet in a single layer (mostly — some overlap is fine).
2. Add the butter, sugar, salt, and enough water to just barely cover the carrots.
3. Bring to a boil over high heat, uncovered.
4. Reduce to a lively simmer and cook until the carrots are TENDER but not falling apart, ~15-25 min depending on cut size. The water should be mostly cooked off by the time the carrots are tender — a bit of trial-and-error to get the timing right on your stove.
5. If the water cooks off before the carrots are done: splash in a bit more and keep going.
6. If the carrots are tender but too much water remains: raise the heat and swirl the pan (don't stir aggressively — you want to keep the carrot shapes intact) to cook it off.
7. FINISH: as the last of the water evaporates, the butter and sugar form a glossy syrup that coats the carrots. Swirl the pan to coat evenly. Salt to taste. Optional herb finish (parsley or tarragon).
notes:
VARIANT A NOTES — JULIA'S BUTTER + SUGAR METHOD

SMALL AMOUNT OF SUGAR — THIS IS A GLAZE, NOT CANDY: Julia's original calls for 1-2 tsp sugar for a pound of carrots. Don't overdo it. Carrots already have natural sugar; the added sugar is there to help form the glaze and reinforce the sweetness, not to make dessert. More sugar than 2 tsp per pound tips into candied-carrot territory, which is a different (and cloying) dish.

UNSALTED BUTTER, GENEROUS: 2-3 tbsp for a pound of carrots. The butter is doing structural work — it's what forms the glossy glaze at the end alongside the reduced water and sugar. Unsalted so you can control the salt separately.

WIDE PAN, SINGLE LAYER: carrots want to sit mostly in a single layer with a bit of overlap tolerated. A wide saucepan or skillet is right; a small deep pot piles the carrots too high and they steam unevenly. If you have too many carrots for one layer in your pan, do two batches.

WATER JUST BARELY COVERS: don't drown the carrots. You want just enough water that the carrots simmer through without needing more added, and so that most of the water is gone by the time the carrots are tender. Too much water and you're boiling; not enough and the carrots don't cook through before the pan burns.

TIMING IS TRIAL-AND-ERROR: the tricky part is landing the moment when the carrots are just tender AND the water is just evaporated. On different stoves and with different cut sizes this varies. Rules of thumb:
- If water evaporates too fast: splash in more.
- If carrots are tender but water remains: raise heat and swirl to reduce.
- If carrots are undercooked and water is nearly gone: add more water and drop the heat slightly.
Don't stress about hitting it perfectly the first time; it's a forgiving technique once you get a feel for your pan.

SWIRL, DON'T STIR: once the water is reducing to glaze territory, swirl the pan rather than stirring with a spoon. Aggressive stirring breaks up the carrot shapes and can smear the glaze around instead of coating. A gentle pan swirl coats evenly and keeps the carrots looking intact.

OPTIONAL HERB FINISH — PARSLEY OR TARRAGON: parsley is the more standard french-provincial finish; tarragon is more elegant and matches the french-chicken-family herb axis. Both work; skip both if serving with a dish that has its own strong herb signature.

CUT SIZE MATTERS FOR TIMING: rounds vs oblique-cut vs matchsticks all cook at different rates. Rounds (~1/4 to 1/2 inch thick) are Julia's default. Oblique cuts (angled slices) present nicer and cook a bit faster because of more surface area. Matchsticks/juliennes cook very fast — 5-8 min instead of 15-25 — and want less water.

=== VARIANT B NOTES — BROTH + BALSAMIC + BROWN SUGAR + THYME ===

VEGETABLE STOCK, NOT WATER: variant B swaps water for vegetable stock — the umami backbone matters more here because there's no butter carrying dairy richness. A good vegetable stock reinforces the carrot flavor and gives the finished glaze more depth. Bouillon-cube water works in a pinch but real stock is better.

BALSAMIC VINEGAR IS THE ACID: this is variant B's biggest departure from Julia's version. The balsamic reduces alongside the sugar to form a sweet-tangy glaze that reads modern-mediterranean rather than french-classical. Don't substitute other vinegars — red wine or apple cider vinegar are too sharp; balsamic's rounded sweetness is what makes the glaze work.

BROWN SUGAR, NOT WHITE: variant B calls for brown sugar (Julia uses white). Brown sugar's molasses depth pairs cleanly with the balsamic and thyme — the trio has a mediterranean-adjacent register that white sugar wouldn't hit. Don't sub white sugar here without knowing the character will shift.

DRIED THYME IS THE HERB: thyme is doing structural aromatic work here, not just garnish. Add during the glaze reduction so the thyme leaves have a moment to bloom in the hot pan. Scatter more on top at serving.

CUT LENGTHWISE — RAINBOW NANTES ARE VISUALLY STRIKING: variant B specifies carrots cut in half LENGTHWISE (not rounds like variant A). This presents beautifully on a plate — long half-cylinders arranged on a serving dish. Rainbow carrots (purple, yellow, orange, white) make the visual pop; regular orange nantes still work but the presentation is less striking.

OIL, NOT BUTTER — VEGAN-FRIENDLY: variant B uses oil (olive or avocado) rather than butter. This is the practical vegan variant, but also just reads cleaner and less rich if you're serving alongside a heavier main.

=== SHARED NOTES ACROSS BOTH VARIANTS ===

BRAISE-THEN-GLAZE TECHNIQUE: both variants follow the same core structure — carrots simmer in liquid until tender, then the liquid reduces to a glaze that coats the carrots. The technique is forgiving; the fat + sugar + reducing liquid combination is what forms the glaze.

CUT AFFECTS TIMING: whether you're doing Julia's rounds or the lengthwise halves of variant B, cut size determines cook time. Adjust up or down from the recipe times based on what you cut.

SWIRL THE PAN AT THE END: same rule as variant A. Once you're in glaze territory, swirling coats evenly without breaking up the carrot shapes.

=== VARIANT B — BROTH + BALSAMIC + BROWN SUGAR + THYME (VEGAN, RUSTIC) ===

A more modern take that leans on vegetable stock, oil (not butter — vegan-friendly), balsamic vinegar's tang, brown sugar's molasses depth, and dried thyme. Cut the carrots LENGTHWISE (halved down the length rather than into rounds) for a rustic-elegant plate presentation — rainbow nantes carrots are visually striking here.

INGREDIENTS:
- 1 cup (250 ml) vegetable stock
- 2.2 lb (1 kg) nantes rainbow carrots, cut in half lengthwise
- 2 tbsp neutral oil (olive oil or avocado oil both work)
- 2 tbsp balsamic vinegar
- 2 tbsp brown sugar
- 2 tsp dried thyme leaves (plus more for serving)
- salt and black pepper to taste

PROCEDURE:
1. In a large skillet, bring the vegetable stock and carrots to a boil.
2. Cover and simmer gently 10-12 min until the carrots are al dente.
3. Uncover and let the liquid reduce until almost dry.
4. Add the oil, balsamic vinegar, brown sugar, and thyme. Continue cooking, stirring, until the carrots are glazed and the liquid has evaporated.
5. Salt and pepper to taste.
6. Transfer to a serving dish; scatter additional thyme leaves on top.
